hard.next_step: 6/19/26 MQ Initial presentation presented. Feedback was positive, pricing is in line with other vendors. Nick said they would evaluate vendor proposals over the next 2 weeks. They will announce shortlisted vendors by July 4th.
hard.ais_why: Next step notes Nicholas was interested in eInvoicing and a demo will be scheduled with the eInvoicing solutions consultant, narrow scope, no AI/Merlin/agentic mention. No customer-voiced AI signal anywhere, defaulting to mild curiosity.
hard.competitor: Undisclosed RFP bidders (multiple); incumbent manual stack (Maximo / OnBase / SAP)

north_star_verdict.headline: Live, champion-driven RFP now sitting on its own down-select: positive 19 Jun proposal feedback and competitive pricing, but the buyer's promised 4 Jul shortlist announcement is 2 days overdue with no word, and the deal is single-threaded to a manager-level contact with no mapped budget owner.
north_star_verdict.math: Today 6 Jul 2026. Created 6 May 2026, ~61 days old. Shortlisted since 5 Jun (~31 days in stage). Close date 6 Aug 2027 = ~396 days out, a placeholder not a real timeline. Cadence was tight: discovery 22 May, demo 5-6 Jun, proposal 19 Jun. Buyer said on 19 Jun they would evaluate over 2 weeks and announce the shortlist by 4 Jul; last real two-way touch was the buyer email 29-30 Jun ('reviewing all bidders proposals, share feedback soon'). The 4 Jul gate has now passed with no recorded outcome, so the deal is awaiting the buyer's own decision, still within days of a stated milestone, not stalled.
evidence: Next Step (19 Jun 2026): 'Feedback was positive, pricing is in line with other vendors. Nick said they would evaluate vendor proposals over the next 2 weeks. They will announce shortlisted vendors by July 4th.' (source: SF Next_Step__c)

Buyer email 29 Jun 2026: 'We have begun the process of reviewing all of our bidders proposals for this project and we are expecting to share feedback on who we will be pursuing soon.' (source: Clari Email Received 29 Jun 2026)

The 4 Jul shortlist-announcement date the buyer set on 19 Jun is now 2 days overdue with no recorded outcome, so the near-term watch is the down-select result, not a slip (source: SF Next_Step__c 19 Jun 2026).

Best Case on a Shortlisted deal with no mapped Economic Buyer, an unverified budget owner, and a 19 Jun amount cut ($146,880 to $127,400) is not defensible; honest category is Pipeline until the shortlist clears and the budget owner is confirmed (source: SF field history 19 Jun 2026 + 0 buyer-side OpportunityContactRole).

metrics.narrative: The buyer has quantified volume but not dollar value. On the 5 Jun demo Metallus confirmed 'approximately 90 to 100,000 invoices a year' as the processing volume, and the goal to 'reduce errors, eliminate manual receipt entries, and improve monthly accuracy and financial visibility'. On the 22 May discovery Nicholas Galbincea described today's state as 'a manual and error-prone process' needing automation for PO-based demand across Maximo, SAP and OnBase for three-way matching and exception handling. The 19 Jun proposal scoped 'automating PO-based invoice processing for ~90k' invoices. The qualitative pain and the volume are clear, but no hard cost, FTE, or savings figure has been put on the table, which is why the forecast can not yet be defended on ROI. Quantifying the cost of staying manual (error rework, FTE hours, late-payment exposure) is the move to harden the business case.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer is mapped or verifiable in Salesforce: the opportunity carries zero OpportunityContactRole entries and no Avoma call was matched this sweep. The only SF-verified buyer contact is Nicholas Galbincea (Manager, Commodity and Supply Chain Modeling per his 30 Jun email signature), who himself asked the pricing-clarification question on 29 Jun and speaks for the evaluation team ('we have begun reviewing all of our bidders proposals'), but a manager-level contact fielding pricing is not evidence of budget ownership. The evidence shows commercial authority sits above Nicholas and is still unnamed. Naming and reaching the budget owner, brokered through Nicholas's live thread rather than a cold approach, is the highest-leverage MEDDPICC move before contracting.

decision_criteria.narrative: The criteria are functional-fit plus price. From discovery and demo the buyer weighs native integration with Maximo, SAP and OnBase, automated three-way matching and exception handling, and a unified flow for both physical-good receipts and service invoices to eliminate dual entry (22 May, 5 Jun). Commercially, the buyer explicitly benchmarked price: on 19 Jun our pricing was reported 'in line with other vendors', so price is a scored criterion and we are competitive but not differentiated on it. What is not yet confirmed is the weighting the buyer applies across fit, price, and implementation cost, and whether AI/Merlin capability is a scored criterion at all (the AIS read shows no customer-voiced AI signal). Confirming the scoring weights with Nicholas is the way to sharpen the finalist pitch.

decision_process.narrative: The process is a formal multi-bidder RFP running to the buyer's own clock. The sequence is visible in the trail: discovery 22 May, demo to '4 primary stakeholders' 5 Jun, proposal delivered 19 Jun, then a stated 2-week evaluation window with a shortlist announcement targeted for 4 Jul. On 29 Jun the buyer confirmed they are 'reviewing all of our bidders proposals' and will 'share feedback on who we will be pursuing soon'. The down-select gate has now passed (6 Jul) with no recorded outcome, so we are inside the buyer's decision window awaiting the result. The gap is the internal approval chain above the evaluation team: who signs off on the shortlist and who authorizes budget is undocumented, and mapping it is the process move once the shortlist lands.

paper_process.narrative: Early contracting mechanics are moving. A mutual NDA is executed: on 29 May the buyer returned 'the attached executed NDA', clearing the path to sample data for integration scoping. A Professional Services scoping session is to be scheduled to define implementation scope and timeline, and Zycus owes a firm implementation cost after that exercise (Marc, 29 Jun: 'The implementation cost estimate is separate from the SaaS fees ... I may be able to get a reduction on the implementation costs after project is [scoped]'). Beyond the NDA and the pending PS scoping there is no visibility yet into MSA/SOW path, legal review, procurement channel, or InfoSec, and as net-new automation (not a renewal) there is no contract-expiration forcing function. Confirming the buyer's contracting and security steps once the shortlist is announced would create that visibility.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is specific and owner-attributed. Nicholas Galbincea described the current invoice processing through Maximo as 'a manual and error-prone process' requiring automation that integrates with Maximo, SAP and OnBase for three-way matching and exception handling (22 May). On 5 Jun the buyer confirmed the goals to 'reduce errors, eliminate manual receipt entries, and improve monthly accuracy and financial visibility' across ~90-100k invoices a year, and to 'centralize both physical good receipts and service invoice processing in a unified solution to eliminate dual entry'. The pain owners are the AR/AP function (Robert Rummer, per prior stakeholder record) and the procurement / commodity team (Nicholas). Confirmed pain, though its dollar cost has not been quantified.

champion.narrative: Nicholas Galbincea is the working champion and the single most engaged buyer thread: he opened the discovery, set the original decision target, is the named point of contact on every proposal touchpoint (22 May, 5 Jun, 17 Jun, 19 Jun), executed the NDA turnaround, and proactively emailed in on 29-30 Jun to keep the pricing dialogue live. He shows advocacy by keeping cadence tight and coordinating internal scheduling. The limitation is seniority and breadth: he is a Manager (Commodity and Supply Chain Modeling), the deal is single-threaded around him at the decision layer with zero OpportunityContactRole entries, and there is no evidence he has access to or has sold internally to the budget owner. 'Developing' because the relationship is real and active but there is no second senior advocate and no confirmation he can carry the shortlist decision. Leaning on his live thread to get the budget-owner intro and to broaden to the Director of Procurement is the next champion move.

competition.narrative: This is a contested, formal RFP with multiple bidders whose names are undisclosed. The buyer confirmed a shortlisting process (22 May), reported our 'pricing is in line with other vendors' (19 Jun), and on 29 Jun said they are 'reviewing all of our bidders proposals' and will announce who they pursue, so several competitors are confirmed but none are named. The incumbent alternative is the manual status quo (Maximo + OnBase + SAP); Metallus will not leave Maximo, so we must integrate with and automate around it rather than displace it. eInvoicing is table-stakes across all bidders, so Merlin (negotiation agent, supplier price-history intelligence, contract-anomaly detection) is the wedge. The live risk is that we do not yet know who else is shortlisted or the criteria weighting; capturing that from Nicholas before the finalist pitch is the competitive move.

deal_movement.summary: Reads the pulse from Next_Step_History, field history, and the 29-30 Jun buyer email: proposal delivered 19 Jun with positive feedback and competitive pricing, amount cut $146,880 to $127,400 the same day, and the buyer now in a self-run 2-week evaluation whose 4 Jul shortlist date has just passed without an announced outcome.

items.change: Proposal presented to the evaluation group; Showpad shared site with the demo recording created as requested
Amount revised down from $146,880 to $127,400 as scope narrowed to PO-based invoice processing
Buyer confirmed a 2-week proposal evaluation and a 4 Jul shortlist-announcement date
Buyer emailed in: 'reviewing all of our bidders proposals, share feedback on who we will be pursuing soon'
Zycus clarified implementation cost is separate from SaaS fees and flagged a possible implementation-cost reduction after scoping

competitive_position.summary: Contested RFP with the field still undisclosed and now at its down-select: as of 19 Jun our pricing is 'in line with other vendors', so we are commercially competitive but not differentiated on price. The strongest current threat is the set of undisclosed rival bidders the buyer is actively choosing among (confirmed 29 Jun); the incumbent manual stack (Maximo + OnBase + SAP) is the status-quo alternative Metallus will not fully leave, so we win by integrating with it. The highest-leverage unknown is the identity and criteria weighting of the other shortlisted vendors, and Nicholas's live thread is the opening to ask now that the 4 Jul gate has passed.

competitors.how_we_win: Use Nicholas's live thread to learn who else is shortlisted and the criteria weighting; lead the finalist case with Merlin's negotiation agent, supplier price-history intelligence and contract-anomaly detection plus the unified physical-plus-service invoice flow, not price.

competitors.quote: Feedback was positive, pricing is in line with other vendors.
competitors.how_we_win: Convert 'in line' into 'clearly better value' by quantifying the manual-process cost and pricing the Merlin AI layer as included differentiation rather than a premium add-on.

competitors.name: Incumbent manual process (Maximo / OnBase / SAP, manual AP)
competitors.quote: The current invoice processing through Maximo is a manual and error-prone process, necessitating automation for efficient three-way matching and exception handling.
competitors.how_we_win: Position as the AI three-way-match and exception engine that integrates with (not replaces) Maximo and SAP and only replaces OnBase approval routing; quantify the error and rework cost of staying manual.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Metallus expects native integration with its Maximo/SAP/OnBase stack, automated three-way matching and exception handling, a unified physical-plus-service invoice flow, and pricing competitive with the other bidders. We are aligned on fit and competitive on price; the exposure is that we are not yet differentiated commercially and the AI/Merlin value is not yet a buyer-scored criterion.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win levers now that the deal sits on its own down-select: break out of the single-thread to reach power, make Merlin the differentiator over table-stakes eInvoicing, and read the 19 Jun scope/amount cut as a defensiveness signal to manage.
flags: Multi-thread now: single-threaded to Nicholas (manager, non-EB) with zero contact roles; map the four 5 Jun demo stakeholders and reach the budget owner before the finalist pitch.

Differentiate on Merlin: eInvoicing is table-stakes across all bidders, so lead the finalist case with the Merlin negotiation agent, supplier price-history intelligence and contract-anomaly detection.
Manage the scope-shrink: the 19 Jun cut ($146,880 to $127,400, PO-based focus) signals cost-containment; confirm phasing intent and protect the Merlin module from being dropped.

critical_signals.text: The 19 Jun proposal review landed well: feedback was positive and pricing came back in line with the other vendors, keeping us alive into the down-select.
Metallus is actively choosing among several undisclosed bidders and the promised 4 Jul shortlist call is now two days late with no word.
The deal was cut from about $147k to $127k on 19 Jun as scope narrowed to PO-based invoice processing, a cost-containment signal to watch.
We remain single-threaded to Nicholas, a manager-level contact who fields pricing himself, with no budget owner identified or reached.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 4.0 (Moderate): Merlin is in scope but no customer-voiced AI or agentic signal appears in any call, email, or next step, so appetite is mild curiosity rather than active pull. No call evidence this sweep to revise it up or down.

Amount cut $146,880 to $127,400 on 19 Jun with scope narrowed to PO-based invoicing, a cost-containment / defensive signal.
Our pricing is only 'in line with other vendors', so we are competitive but not differentiated commercially.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is live but rep-led into a buyer decision window: the last genuine two-way touch was the buyer email 29-30 Jun, and the buyer's own 4 Jul shortlist date has passed without an announced outcome, so the read is 'awaiting the down-select', not cooling.
items: Buyer proactively emailed in on 29-30 Jun (pricing clarification and bidder-review update).
The 4 Jul shortlist-announcement date the buyer set has passed with no recorded outcome as of 6 Jul.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: We are a live finalist in a multi-bidder eInvoicing RFP that has just hit its own 4 Jul down-select: the 19 Jun proposal landed well and pricing is competitive, but the deal is single-threaded to a manager-level contact, no budget owner is mapped, and the amount was cut $19k the same day, so the win hangs on reaching power and differentiating on Merlin before the finalist call.

win_position.text: Scores in the mid-50s and held below the Shortlisted cap because, while the 19 Jun proposal drew positive feedback and competitive pricing, no Economic Buyer is mapped, the deal is single-threaded to a manager, and the amount was just cut $19k.
We are a confirmed live bidder with strong functional fit: Nicholas Galbincea called the feedback positive and pricing 'in line with other vendors' on 19 Jun, and the NDA is executed.
We are competitive but not differentiated on price against undisclosed rivals, and Merlin's AI value is not yet a buyer-scored criterion (AIS 4.0, no customer-voiced AI signal).
The 19 Jun cut from $146,880 to $127,400 with scope narrowed to PO-based invoicing is a cost-containment signal that could shrink or slow the deal.

deal_momentum.text: Tight cadence held from discovery (22 May) to demo (5 Jun) to proposal (19 Jun), and the buyer named concrete dates (2-week eval, 4 Jul shortlist).
The buyer's promised 4 Jul shortlist announcement is now 2 days overdue with no recorded outcome, so momentum depends on a decision we do not yet control.

customer_commitment.text: Metallus executed the NDA (29 May), attended a 4-stakeholder demo, and Nicholas proactively emailed in on 29-30 Jun on pricing.
Commitment is concentrated in one manager-level contact with no budget-owner sponsorship and no second senior advocate.

deal_risk.text: No Economic Buyer is mapped and the opportunity carries zero buyer-side contact roles, so we cannot yet reach or align the person who authorizes spend.
A multi-bidder RFP where we are only price-competitive, plus a fresh scope/amount cut, means the award could flip on differentiation we have not yet landed.

customer_preference.evidence: Positive proposal feedback and competitive pricing (19 Jun), but no explicit preference for Zycus over the other bidders.

competitive_pressure.evidence: Active multi-bidder RFP with us only 'in line' on price and undisclosed rivals (29 Jun).

scope_trend.evidence: Amount cut $146,880 to $127,400 on 19 Jun as scope narrowed to PO-based invoicing.

scope_change.detail: On 19 Jun the amount was cut from $146,880 to $127,400 and the 19 Jun proposal narrowed the scope to 'automating PO-based invoice processing for ~90k' invoices/year. The buyer appears to be containing cost and may want a phased entry rather than a full-suite deployment; protect the Merlin AI module from being dropped as scope tightens.

customer_preference.evidence: Feedback was positive and pricing 'in line with other vendors' (19 Jun), and Nicholas kept the dialogue live, but no explicit preference for Zycus over rivals was stated.

business_case.evidence: Volume quantified (~90-100k invoices/year) and pain clear (manual, error-prone, dual entry), but no dollar cost, FTE, or savings figure articulated by the customer.
